context: Turn to the book of Ephesians, if you will, and chapter number 6
point_summary: Ephesians 6:10 is invoked as a call to strength and courage for faithful ministry amid adversity and wickedness, paralleling the example of Elisha's surrendered life.

reference_text: Ephesians 6 and beginning in verse number 10
context: The Bible says in Ephesians 6 and beginning in verse number 10, Finally, my brethren, be strong in the Lord and the power of His might.
point_summary: Ephesians 6:10 grounds the call to strength and courage in the Lord, drawn as the New Testament parallel to Elisha's faithful endurance amid adversity and spiritual warfare.

context: Wherefore, take unto you the whole armor of God, notice that ye may be able to withstand in the evil day, and having done all, to stand.
point_summary: Putting on the whole armor of God equips believers to withstand in the evil day—a repeated emphasis on standing that frames the Christian life as active spiritual warfare requiring strength and endurance.

context: Stand therefore, having your loins girded out with truth, and having on the breastplate of righteousness,
point_summary: Girding one's loins with truth and donning the breastplate of righteousness (v. 14) are part of the full armor enabling believers to stand and withstand in the present evil day of spiritual warfare.

context: and your feet shod with the preparation of the gospel of peace,
point_summary: Readiness for spiritual warfare — symbolized by feet shod with the gospel of peace — is one piece of the full armor God provides so believers can stand firm in an evil day.

context: above all, taking the shield of faith, wherewith ye shall be able to quench all the fiery darts of the wicked,
point_summary: Faith is the specific piece of armor that quenches every fiery dart of the wicked, making it indispensable to standing firm in the spiritual warfare believers face in this evil day.

context: and take the helmet of salvation, and the sword of the Spirit, which is the Word of God.
point_summary: Ephesians 6:10–17 is read as a unit to establish that believers are called to stand and withstand in spiritual warfare by putting on the whole armor of God.

context: we have this instruction in Ephesians 6 with regard to the spiritual warfare that we're engaged in, and that we're to be strong in the Lord, and the power of His might, to put on the whole armor of God.
point_summary: Ephesians 6's repeated call to "stand" and "withstand" is pressed as the New Testament believer's charge to hold firm on truth against cultural and spiritual pressure without compromise.

context: Not only that the New Testament principle of standing according to Ephesians 6, but this word in 2 Timothy 3, continue.
point_summary: The New Testament principle of standing, drawn from Ephesians 6, is invoked as the biblical mandate for courageous, unwavering faithfulness rather than capitulating to cultural pressure.

reference_text: put on the whole armor of God
context: you need to to follow the admonition of God in his word, that we put on the whole armor of God and understand that we're to be strong in the Lord
point_summary: Putting on the whole armor of God is the necessary means by which believers purpose to stand faithfully unto the end, even amid persistent opposition.

context: Because the Word of God says that I'm to love my wife as Christ also loved the church and gave himself for it.
point_summary: Wholehearted love for one's wife, modeled on Christ's self-giving love for the church, demands total investment rather than half-hearted devotion in marriage and family.

context: Notice in Ephesians chapter number six, in Ephesians six and beginning in verse number five, notice what the Bible says.
point_summary: Joseph's faithful, trustworthy conduct as Potiphar's overseer is shown to embody the New Testament standard of Ephesians 6:5—serving earthly masters with singleness of heart "as unto Christ"—centuries before that command was written.

context: Servants, be obedient to them that are your masters according to the flesh, with fear and trembling, in singleness of your heart, as unto Christ.
point_summary: Joseph's life in Genesis 39 is held up as a living illustration of Ephesians 6:5, demonstrating that faithful, wholehearted service rendered "as unto Christ" transcends any master-servant or employer-employee context.

context: Not with eyeservice, as menpleasers; but as the servants of Christ, doing the will of God from the heart;
point_summary: Faithful service in any employment context is to be rendered not as eye-service to please men but as unto Christ, doing God's will from the heart.

context: I am mindful of the truth of Ephesians 5 in verse number 10,
point_summary: Ephesians 5:10 is brought to bear on Micah 6:8's call to "do justly," framing the Christian life as an ongoing process of proving—demonstrating—what is well-pleasing to God.

context: proving what is acceptable in the sight of the Lord. And the fact that we would understand this truth of do justly. Am I doing the right thing? Do I have the pure motives? We find that in Ephesians 5 in verse number 10, proving what is acceptable.
point_summary: Proving what is acceptable to God—the word meaning "well-pleasing"—is an active, refining process of measuring conduct against God's standard, not one's own, illustrated through Micah's call to do justly and love mercy.

context: Ephesians 2 talks about the fact that He's rich, God who is rich in mercy.
point_summary: Humility before God is grounded in remembering that redemption was entirely God's act—He who is rich in mercy rescued us when we were lost and undone.

context: I'm reminded of the truth of Ephesians 5, and verse number 16, where we're told that we are to redeem the time because the days are evil.
point_summary: The imminent return of Christ makes every day count, calling believers to redeem the time by engaging fully in the work God has assigned them before he comes.

context: that you put off concerning the former conversation, the old man which is corrupt according to the deceitful lust
point_summary: Ephesians 4:22 grounds the call to forgive by invoking the "put off the old man" principle—the believer's former manner of life, driven by deceitful lusts, is to be laid aside in favor of renewed conduct.

context: let all bitterness and wrath and anger and clamor and evil speaking be put away from you with all malice
point_summary: Ephesians 4:31's command to put away bitterness is presented as the "put off" side of Paul's broader put-off/put-on principle, with forgiveness of others grounded in God's prior forgiveness of believers.

context: and be kind one to another, tenderhearted, forgiving one another. And then he tells us this at the end of the verse, and it's so important that we understand this perspective really that is given to us at the end of verse number 32, even as God, for Christ's sake, hath forgiven you
point_summary: Ephesians 4:32 grounds the command to forgive others in the prior reality of God's forgiveness through Christ, illustrating Paul's "put off, put on" principle by replacing bitterness with kindness and tenderheartedness.

context: Paul dealt with their former conversation that they were dead in their trespasses and sins, and yet God, who is rich in mercy for his great love wherewith he had loved us
point_summary: Ephesians 2's account of believers once being dead in trespasses yet forgiven by a mercy-rich God grounds the Ephesians 4:32 command to forgive others "even as God, for Christ's sake, hath forgiven you."

context: even as God, for Christ's sake, hath forgiven you
point_summary: Forgiveness of others is grounded in the prior reality of God's own forgiveness of us in Christ, making divine mercy the model and motivation for horizontal forgiveness.

reference_text: even as God, for Christ's sake, hath forgiven you
context: ...that we would be forgiving. Remember, even as God, for Christ's sake, hath forgiven you, I'm so thankful...
point_summary: Forgiveness toward others is grounded in the prior reality that God, for Christ's sake, has already forgiven believers—making Christlikeness the standard and motive for a forgiving spirit.

context: Ephesians you look at the passages throughout the book and you find that it is absolutely hammered home in Ephesians 1 through 3 that if you've trusted Christ your Savior you are his child you are secure in Christ
point_summary: Identity and security in Christ are foundational in Ephesians 1–3, grounding the subsequent call to walk worthy rather than as unbelievers.

context: Remember what Paul says in the book of Ephesians in chapter number five. Proving what is acceptable unto the Lord.
point_summary: Ephesians 5's call to prove what is acceptable to the Lord is cited as the scriptural standard for a sanctified, God-pleasing walk that flows from the atonement.

context: we've been given the responsibility in the book of Ephesians in the fourth chapter that we are to speak the truth in love
point_summary: The church's mandate from Ephesians 4 to speak the truth in love is invoked as the standard the American church has abandoned by losing its voice on life, marriage, and gender.

context: Notice in Ephesians, what he says in Ephesians 6, as he's, after he gives all of the information with regard to the armor of God, and he encourages
point_summary: Paul's repeated requests for prayer for bold gospel proclamation—seen in both Colossians 4 and Ephesians 6:19—demonstrate that even the most fearless evangelist depends on intercession for Spirit-given utterance.

context: We find that the apostle Paul as he's writing to the church in Ephesus in Ephesians chapter 6 in verse number 20 he refers to himself as being an ambassador in bonds
point_summary: Paul's self-description as "an ambassador in bonds" in Ephesians 6:20 is cited as one of only two New Testament uses of the word "ambassador," illustrating that imprisonment did not nullify his ambassadorial calling or gospel witness.

context: as we look at the apostle Paul making that statement in Ephesians 6 that he was an ambassador in bonds
point_summary: Paul's self-description in Ephesians 6 as "an ambassador in bonds" is cited as one biblical use of the word "ambassador," providing the textual bridge to the sermon's central claim in 2 Corinthians 5 that believers are ambassadors for Christ.

context: Ephesians chapter two, notice what the Bible says here in Ephesians chapter two, as we think about this truth of reconciliation.
point_summary: Ephesians 2 is read to show that humanity's natural state—spiritual death and enmity toward God—makes reconciliation not merely desirable but absolutely necessary.

context: In verse number one, the Bible says, and you hath he quickened who were dead in trespasses and sins.
point_summary: Spiritual death before salvation — being dead in trespasses and sins — establishes the desperate human condition from which reconciliation rescues the believer, who is then made alive in Christ.

context: Verse two, wherein in time past, you walked according to the course of this world, according to the prince, the power of the air, the spirit that now worketh in the children of disobedience,
point_summary: Before reconciliation, every person walked under the dominion of the prince of the power of the air, spiritually dead and living in disobedience — the very condition from which God's reconciling grace rescues sinners.

context: among whom also we all had our conversation in times past in the lust of our flesh, fulfilling the desires of the flesh and of the mind, and were by nature the children of wrath, even as others.
point_summary: Ephesians 2:3 is read as part of a continuous passage establishing humanity's pre-salvation condition—dead in sin, enslaved to fleshly desires, and under God's wrath—against which the "but God" of reconciliation stands.

context: But God, aren't you thankful for the but God? But God who is rich in mercy for his great love, wherewith he loved us,
point_summary: God's mercy and love toward sinners who were spiritually dead—the "but God" of Ephesians 2:4—grounds the reconciliation that makes peace with God possible through Christ.

context: even when we were dead in sin.
point_summary: Reconciliation with God is entirely by grace, not human merit — those once spiritually dead have been made alive together with Christ, establishing peace with God through imputed righteousness alone.

context: hath quickened us together with Christ, by grace ye are saved
point_summary: Even when spiritually dead in sin, God by grace made believers alive together with Christ — salvation presented here as a miraculous, instantaneous new birth, not a gradual process.

context: and hath raised us up together and made us to sit in heavenly places in Christ Jesus
point_summary: Believers who were once spiritually dead and without Christ have been raised and seated in heavenly places in Christ Jesus entirely by God's grace, not by their own works.

context: that in the ages to come he might show the exceeding riches of his grace and his kindness toward us through Christ Jesus
point_summary: God's future display of grace toward believers across coming ages is cited as the backdrop for understanding the magnitude of what reconciliation accomplishes for those once dead in sin.

context: For by grace are you saved through faith, and that not of yourselves, but of the gift of God
point_summary: Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith in a moment, not a process or human achievement, as the passage through verse 10 establishes before the sermon moves to verse 11.

context: not of works, lest any man should boast
point_summary: Salvation is not earned by works but is God's gift of grace, lest any man boast—a truth the sermon grounds in the broader Ephesians 2 passage read aloud without extended comment.

context: For we are his workmanship created in Christ Jesus unto good works, which God hath before ordained, that we should walk in them
point_summary: Salvation's purpose is not merely rescue from spiritual death but creation for good works, as believers are God's workmanship ordained to walk in them.

context: Wherefore remember, remember, remember what? That ye, being in time past Gentiles in the flesh
point_summary: Ephesians 2:11's call to "remember" grounds the appeal that every believer can identify a specific moment before salvation when they were without Christ, without hope, and without peace.

context: that at that time, notice what it says, ye were without Christ
point_summary: Before salvation, the unregenerate were utterly without Christ—a condition the sermon grounds in personal memory to underscore that conversion is a definite moment, not a gradual process.

context: but now, in Christ Jesus, those that have been ye who sometimes were far off are made nigh by the blood of Christ.
point_summary: The radical reversal of "but now" in Ephesians 2:13 marks the transition from a past state of being far off and without Christ to being brought near by His blood.

context: For he, the Lord Jesus Christ, for he is our peace, who hath made both one and hath broken down the middle wall
point_summary: Christ himself is identified as our peace who broke down the dividing wall, reconciling formerly separated peoples to God through his flesh.

context: having abolished in his flesh, notice, the enmity, even the law of commandments contained in ordinances, for to make in himself of twain one new man, so making peace
point_summary: Christ's abolishing of the law's ordinances in His flesh broke down the dividing wall between Jew and Gentile, creating one new man and establishing the peace that grounds the believer's ministry of reconciliation.

context: and that he might reconcile both unto God in one body by the cross, having slain the enmity thereby
point_summary: Reconciliation with God—achieved through Christ's cross by slaying the enmity and uniting both Jew and Gentile in one body—is the doctrinal ground for the believer's ministry of reconciliation as Christ's ambassador.

context: By the way, we understand that Jesus Christ gave himself for the church. Amen? Ephesians chapter number five.
point_summary: Christ's sacrificial giving of himself for the church, cited from Ephesians 5, grounds the claim that the church belongs to God and that he alone has the right to dictate how it functions.

context: in Ephesians four and verse number 11, where he says, and he gave some apostles, but then he says pastors and teachers.
point_summary: The singular New Testament use of "pastor" in Ephesians 4:11 is cited to establish that the pastoral role is fundamentally one of shepherding and feeding the flock.

context: And notice what he says in Ephesians 4 in verse number 3.
point_summary: Unity between historically divided peoples like Jews and Gentiles is only possible through Christ, making the deliberate effort commanded in Ephesians 4:3 to "keep the unity of the spirit in the bond of peace" both necessary and urgent.

context: endeavoring to keep the unity of the spirit
point_summary: Unity of the Spirit in Ephesians 4:3 is cited as the starting point of the chapter's instruction, framing the call to resist the devil as inseparable from maintaining that God-commanded unity.

context: I'm going to get back to verse number 26 here in just a moment
point_summary: Ephesians 4:26 is cited in connection with verse 27's warning against giving the devil a foothold, framing unresolved conflict as an opening Satan exploits toward a stronghold.

context: neither give place to the devil
point_summary: Giving Satan even a small opening — a "toehold" — enables him to gain a foothold and ultimately a stronghold, so believers must yield him no place whatsoever amid conflict.

context: endeavoring to keep the unity of the spirit and the bond of peace
point_summary: Resolving conflict quickly is grounded in Ephesians 4:3's call to *endeavor*—a word the sermon unpacks as diligent, urgent labor to keep the unity of the Spirit in the bond of peace.

context: be angry and sin not
point_summary: Anger is acknowledged as a legitimate human emotion, but the text's command — "be angry and sin not" — is pressed as a difficult, often-violated standard that demands resolving conflict quickly before sin takes hold.

context: neither give place to the devil
point_summary: Allowing anger to replay unchecked in the mind gives the enemy a foothold, making swift conflict resolution necessary to avoid the sin that unresolved anger produces.

context: be angry and sin not. Anger is an emotion that we have as people, as human beings, it's an emotion that we have.
point_summary: Anger is a permissible emotion, but unresolved wrath that lingers past sundown opens a foothold to Satan, making swift conflict resolution a spiritual necessity.

context: let not the sun go down upon your wrath, neither give place to the devil.
point_summary: Allowing anger to linger unresolved gives the devil a foothold, making swift conflict resolution essential to guarding unity among believers.

context: let not the sun go down upon your wrath
point_summary: Unresolved anger, left to fester rather than addressed quickly, gives the devil a foothold and turns minor conflicts into larger, more destructive divisions.

context: neither give place to the devil
point_summary: Unresolved conflict gives the devil a foothold, as lingering wrath festers into larger division that Satan actively exploits within homes, families, and congregations.

context: proving what is acceptable unto the Lord. In Ephesians 5, in verse 10.
point_summary: Ephesians 5:10's call to "prove what is acceptable unto the Lord" is cited as the scriptural basis for using God's Word as the standard of self-examination.

context: but speaking the truth in love may grow up into him in all things which is the head, even Christ.

context: 'Wherefore, putting away lying, speak every man truth with his neighbor, for we are members one of another.'

context: It's a principle that you find in Ephesians 4 and also in Colossians 3. And it's this principle, put off, put on.

context: Notice in Ephesians 5 and verse 26, that he might sanctify and cleanse it with the washing of water by the Word.

context: We read about the cleansing in verse number 26

context: in verse number 27 he says that he might present it to himself, he's speaking about the church, a glorious church, not having spot or wrinkle or any such thing, but that it should be holy and without blemish

context: ...we just looked at that just a moment ago in Ephesians 5 and we see that that example...

context: Ephesians chapter number one talks to us about the fact that he brought our eternal salvation

context: If you look at Ephesians chapter number one, you'll notice within the text of scripture how that God reveals to us in that portion the plan for redemption, that there's only one way for man to be redeemed.
point_summary: Ephesians 1 is read not as a decree about who is elected but as God's predetermined plan revealing the singular how of salvation — through Jesus Christ and his blood.

context: for by grace are you saved through faith and that not of yourselves is the gift of God
point_summary: Salvation by grace through faith alone excludes works both as the means of being saved and as the means of staying saved, grounding the believer's security entirely in God's gift rather than human effort.

context: not of works, lest any man should boast
point_summary: Salvation is entirely by grace through faith, not of works, ruling out any grace-plus-works system as a basis for being saved or staying saved.

context: for by grace are you saved through faith and that not of yourselves is the gift of God
point_summary: Saving grace (Eph. 2:8) is distinguished here from "serving grace"—the ongoing divine enablement God supplies to accomplish what He has called His already-redeemed people to do.

context: not of works lest any man should boast
point_summary: Saving grace, grounded in Ephesians 2:8–9, is distinguished here from a continuing "serving grace" — the divine enablement God supplies for believers already saved to fulfill what He has called them to do.

context: We see it in Ephesians chapter 1.

context: And he says that we have been given the Holy Spirit, which is the earnest of our inheritance, Ephesians 1, verses 12 through 14.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: And we ended last Wednesday evening by looking at that verse of Scripture where the Bible says and be kind one to another, tenderhearted, forgiving one another, even as God, for Christ's sake, hath forgiven you.

context: Our God is able to do exceeding abundantly above all that we ask or think.

context: He is able to do exceeding abundantly above all that we ask or think, according to Ephesians 3 and verse number 20.
point_summary: God's ability to work miraculously beyond human comprehension in 2 Kings 7 is anchored in the truth that He does exceeding abundantly above all we ask or think.

context: Ephesians chapter number 2, if you read the first several verses of that chapter, the Bible speaks about how we all had our conversation in time past.

context: be strong in the Lord and in the power of his might

context: we wrestle not against flesh and blood, but against spiritual wickedness in high places
point_summary: Spiritual warfare demands divine strength, as Paul's reference to wrestling against spiritual wickedness in high places underscores the believer's total dependence on God's power rather than human resources.

context: he's strengthened you in the inner man
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Be strong in the Lord and the power of his might
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: for by grace are you saved through faith. And that not of yourselves, it is the gift of God
point_summary: Salvation by grace through faith apart of works, cited alongside verse 9, frames the foundation from which verse 10's call to good works flows.

context: not of works, lest any man should boast
point_summary: Salvation is entirely God's gift, not human achievement — a foundation the sermon establishes from verses 8–9 before pivoting to verse 10's call to Spirit-empowered service.

context: for we are his workmanship, created in Christ Jesus unto good works, which God hath before ordained that we should walk in them
point_summary: Created in Christ Jesus unto good works, Ephesians 2:10 grounds Christian service not in human initiative but in God's prior ordination, framing all service as rendered ultimately to God alone.

context: for by grace are you saved through faith, and that not of yourselves, it is the gift of God
point_summary: Salvation is entirely by grace through faith — not by works — establishing that Christian service flows from gratitude for a gift already received, not as a means to earn or maintain salvation.

context: not of works, lest any man should boast
point_summary: Salvation is entirely by grace through faith—not by works—meaning service to God is neither the means of getting saved nor of staying saved.

context: and we understand this morning that we are his workmanship created in Christ Jesus notice unto good works. And then at the end of verse number 10 of Ephesians 2 it says, which God hath before ordained that we should walk in them.
point_summary: Believers are called to rejoice in whatever ability God has currently given them to serve, since the good works of Ephesians 2:10 were ordained by God for each stage of life.

context: Notice in Ephesians four and verse number seven, but unto every
point_summary: Grace distributed individually to every believer—"no one left out"—means God has personally equipped and enabled each saved person to serve Him, which should motivate participation rather than spectating.

context: Wherefore he saith, when he ascended up on high, he led captivity captive and gave gifts unto men.
point_summary: Christ's ascension and gift-giving in Ephesians 4:8 grounds the point that every believer's ability to serve is divinely bestowed, not self-selected, and therefore cause for rejoicing.

context: And he gave some apostles and some prophets and some evangelists and some pastors and teachers
point_summary: Ephesians 4:11's list of gifted offices—apostles, prophets, evangelists, pastors, teachers—is cited to show that God himself gives, equips, and enables every believer for the work of ministry, leaving no one out.

context: In Ephesians 4 we won't take the time to turn there but we spoke in that portion of scripture how the Lord gave some to the church. He gave the evangelists, some apostles, evangelists, pastors, teachers and he said why for the perfecting of the saints
point_summary: Christ's gifting of apostles, evangelists, pastors, and teachers to the church serves the dual purpose of glorifying God and edifying His people.

context: for the perfecting of the saints, for the work of the ministry, for the edifying of the body of Christ
point_summary: Serving God with pure motives edifies the body of Christ, the sermon argues, because gifts exercised for the audience of one—not for human recognition—accomplish their intended purpose of building up God's people.

context: God used the Apostle Paul to write to the church at Ephesus and told them we wrestle not against flesh and blood.
point_summary: Spiritual warfare—not against flesh and blood—frames the entire call to press on, establishing that the equipping needed for endurance is divine and spiritual, not human or carnal.

context: for by grace are you saved through faith, and that not of yourselves, but of the gift of God
point_summary: Scripture's reproof of works-righteousness—illustrated by Ephesians 2:8—is presented as a personal corrective that overturned a pre-conversion belief that good works would secure salvation.

context: not of works, lest any man should boast
point_summary: Scripture's reproof of the "good works" salvation assumption—illustrated by the preacher's pre-conversion belief—is grounded in Ephesians 2:9's declaration that salvation is not of works, correcting false doctrine with God's Word.

context: In whom ye also trusted, after that ye heard the word of truth, the gospel of your salvation.
point_summary: The sealing of believers with the Holy Spirit, received at the moment of trusting the gospel, is presented as the guarantee securing the inheritance that no one can pluck away.

context: In whom also, after that ye believed, ye were sealed with the Holy Spirit of promise.
point_summary: The sealing of believers with the Holy Spirit at the moment of salvation, following their hearing and trusting the gospel, is presented as the guaranteed earnest of their eternal inheritance.

context: Which is the earnest of our inheritance until the redemption of the purchased possession unto the praise of his glory.
point_summary: The indwelling Holy Spirit is presented as the "earnest"—God's down payment guaranteeing the believer's inheritance until Christ returns to claim His purchased possession.

context: for by grace are you saved through faith, that not of yourselves
point_summary: Salvation received by grace through faith—not works—is the very ground that makes subsequent service to God an act of gratitude rather than an attempt to earn or keep what God has already given.

context: For by grace are you saved through faith. And that not of yourselves, it is the gift of God, but not of works, lest any man should boast.
point_summary: Salvation by grace through faith, not works, is cited as the foundation for understanding God's gracious character—the very preciousness being explored throughout the sermon.

context: ...and you hath He quickened who were dead in trespasses and sins.
point_summary: Ephesians 2:1 grounds the sermon's contrast between grace and mercy by establishing that every believer was once spiritually dead in trespasses and sins, deserving only wrath, before God made them alive.

context: be kind one to another, tenderhearted, forgiving one another
point_summary: Believers who have received God's forgiveness in Christ are thereby instructed to extend that same forgiveness to one another.

context: I reference Ephesians four, in verse number 32, where the Bible says, and be kind one to another, tenderhearted, forgiving one another, even as God, for Christ's sake, hath forgiven you
point_summary: Remembering that God has forgiven us in Christ is presented as the motivating power that enables believers to extend forgiveness to others.

context: Ephesians chapter six, if you would, just turn there for a moment.
point_summary: Spiritual warfare against a real adversary—not flesh and blood but principalities and powers—frames why unresolved unforgiveness becomes an opening Satan exploits against believers.

context: Paul writes of this to the church in Ephesus in Ephesians chapter six, beginning in verse number 10, where Paul says, finally, my brethren, be strong in the Lord and the power of his might.
point_summary: Spiritual warfare is real and ongoing, requiring believers to stand armed and prayerful against an enemy who actively seeks advantage over God's people.

context: Put on the whole armor of God that you may be able to stand against the wiles of the devil.
point_summary: Withholding forgiveness opens a foothold for Satan, making the whole-armor readiness of Ephesians 6 necessary for God's people engaged in real spiritual warfare against a real adversary.

context: Notice, for we wrestle not against flesh and blood, but against principalities, against powers, against the rulers of the darkness of this world, against spiritual wickedness in high places.
point_summary: Spiritual warfare is not against human opponents but against unseen principalities and powers, establishing why Satan's devices in 2 Corinthians 2:11 must be taken seriously by the church.

context: He says in verse 13, wherefore, take unto you the whole armor of God that you may be able to withstand in the evil day, and having done all to stand, stand therefore, having your loins girt about with truth
point_summary: Withstanding the enemy's schemes in "the evil day" requires the full armor of God, underscoring that the Christian life is spiritual warfare demanding complete divine equipment.

context: notice in Ephesians chapter number 4

point_summary: Ephesians 4's "put off / put on" language (vv. 22ff.) is applied as the New Testament counterpart to the priestly garments of Exodus 29, illustrating the believer-priest's call to consecrated conduct after positional salvation.

context: in Ephesians 1 and verse 13, in whom ye also
point_summary: Ephesians 1:13 marks the transition point in the letter where positional truth (salvation received through hearing and trusting the gospel) gives way to practical instruction on how believers are to walk and live.

context: Pick it up with me in Ephesians four in verse number 22, we begin to see this principle, it's also mentioned in the book of Colossians, of put off and put on. In verse number 22, he says this, that ye put off concerning the former conversation the old man, which is corrupt according to the deceitful lust, and be renewed in the spirit of your mind.
point_summary: Ephesians 4:22's command to "put off the old man" illustrates the practical, sanctifying dimension of the Christian life that follows salvation, paralleling the priestly robing in Exodus 29.

context: Be angry and sin not, let not the sun go down upon your wrath, neither give place to the devil
point_summary: Anger is treated not as a sin to be eliminated but as an emotion to be governed—believers must not let it linger past sundown or yield ground to the devil.

context: let him that stole steal no more
point_summary: Stealing is presented as one concrete example in the "put off / put on" pattern of Ephesians 4, replaced not merely by honest restraint but by productive labor aimed at generosity toward those in need.

context: let no corrupt communication proceed out of your mouth, but that which is good to the use of edifying, that it may minister grace unto the hearers
point_summary: Ephesians 4:29 is presented as the "put off / put on" pattern applied to speech: corrupt communication belongs to the old man and must be replaced by words that edify and minister grace.

context: putting away lying
point_summary: Lying, as a behavior natural to fallen humanity, is something believers must actively "put off" in consecrated living, replacing it with truthfulness as part of the ongoing pattern of sanctification.

context: remember the Bible says there in Ephesians 4 as we studied several weeks ago now that we're to be angry and sin not
point_summary: Unresolved anger that is not checked, as Ephesians 4 warns, does not solve problems but escalates them—illustrated here by Cain's murder of Abel accomplishing nothing but greater judgment.

context: we are children of the light. We are to walk as children of light

context: husbands, love your wives, even as Christ so loved the church and gave himself for it
point_summary: Ephesians 5:25 is cited as the lead-in to verse 26, where Christ's self-giving love for the church is the basis for its ongoing sanctification and cleansing through the Word.

context: he might sanctify and cleanse it with the washing of water by the word
point_summary: Sanctification from sin's defilement comes through the Word of God, which continually cleanses believers as Christ's bride, presenting her holy and without blemish before him.

context: a glorious church, not having spot or wrinkle or any such thing, but that it should be holy and without blemish
point_summary: Cleansing from worldly defilement comes through the Word of God, which sanctifies the church so Christ may present her holy and without blemish.

context: being cleansed by the Word of God and the work of the Spirit of God
point_summary: The Word of God, applied by the Spirit, is the cleansing agent that keeps believers unspotted from the world and maintains sweet fellowship with the Savior.

context: He is able, the Bible says that He's able to do exceeding abundantly above
point_summary: God's limitless ability is held up as the reason prayer to Him is never futile, contrasting the silence experienced by those who cry out to gods who can do nothing.

context: for by grace we are saved through faith, Ephesians 2, 8, and 9
point_summary: Salvation by grace through faith (Eph. 2:8–9) removes the believer as his own standard, freeing him from the misery of never knowing if he has done enough to earn heaven.

context: for by grace we are saved through faith, Ephesians 2, 8, and 9
point_summary: Salvation by grace through faith alone (Eph. 2:8–9) removes human merit as the standard, freeing believers from the misery of never knowing if they had done enough to earn heaven.

context: we are his workmanship created in Christ Jesus, that we are to work and we are to live and to walk in good works
point_summary: Salvation by grace (Eph. 2:8–9) issues in good works (v. 10) not as the ground of heaven but as the evidence of it, with God himself as the one working in the believer to produce that fruit.

context: also in the book of Ephesians

context: Ephesians chapter number two, beginning in verse number 11. He says, wherefore, remember
point_summary: Ephesians 2:11–12 is read as a portrait of the believer's pre-salvation condition—alienated, without Christ, without hope—establishing the absence of peace that Jehovah Shalom then answers.

context: for by grace are you saved through faith. He mentions in verse number eight
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: verses 11 and 12, as we consider this thought of the absence of peace
point_summary: Before salvation, Gentiles were utterly without Christ, alienated from Israel's covenants, without hope and without God — a condition the sermon identifies as the very definition of the absence of peace.

context: ye, being in time past Gentiles in the flesh, who are called uncircumcision, by that which is called the circumcision in the flesh made by hands, that at that time ye were without Christ, being aliens from the commonwealth of Israel and strangers from the covenants of promise, having no hope and without God in the world
point_summary: The pre-salvation condition described in Ephesians 2:11–12—alienation from Christ, Israel's commonwealth, and the covenants—implies an absence of peace that verse 14's declaration of Christ as "our peace" directly answers.

context: He is our peace in verse number 14
point_summary: Christ himself being our peace (v. 14) is the resolution to the pre-salvation condition of alienation, hopelessness, and absence of peace described in verses 11–12.

context: Over the book of Ephesians, and we looked at verses 11 and 12 earlier, but look at verse 13 and following with me
point_summary: Peace with God, once absent and then announced through the gospel, is now a settled assurance the believer possesses—grounded not in personal merit but in justification by faith through Christ.

context: verse 13, but now in Christ Jesus, ye who sometimes were far off are made nigh by the blood of Christ
point_summary: Peace with God, once absent because of sin and enmity, is now secured "by the blood of Christ," who is himself our peace, having abolished the enmity through his flesh.

context: for he, Jesus Christ, is our peace. Who hath made both one and hath broken down the middle wall of partition between us
point_summary: Christ himself is identified as our peace — the one who broke down the wall of partition caused by sin and enmity between humanity and God.

context: having abolished in his flesh, the enmity, even the law of commandments
point_summary: Christ's abolishing of the enmity through His flesh is presented as the decisive act by which God made peace between sinful humanity and Himself, fulfilling His name Jehovah Shalom.

context: contained in ordinances for to make in himself of twain one new man, so making what? Making peace
point_summary: Christ abolished in His flesh the enmity caused by sin and the law, reconciling formerly estranged sinners to God and making peace through the cross.

context: And then he might reconcile both unto God in one body by the cross, having slain the enmity thereby
point_summary: Christ's cross-work reconciled sinners to God by slaying the enmity caused by sin, making peace between those once far off and God through one body.

context: and came and preached peace to you, which were far off and to them that were nigh
point_summary: Christ's proclamation of peace to those "far off" and those "near" is identified as the gospel announcement that brings both groups into reconciled standing with God through the cross.

context: now therefore ye are no more strangers and foreigners, but fellow citizens with the saints and with the household of God
point_summary: Those once alienated from God are now, through Christ's peacemaking work on the cross, no longer strangers but fellow citizens in the household of God.

context: the body is fitly framed together and every member has their place of service
point_summary: The body of Christ is fitly joined together so that every member has a place of service, meaning believers are not self-sufficient islands but God-ordained partners in His work.

context: God is able to do, exceeding abundantly above all that we ask or think
point_summary: God's capacity to do "exceeding abundantly above all that we ask or think" is illustrated by the miraculous catch that left seasoned fishermen astonished and their boats sinking under the weight of the haul.

context: God is able to do exceeding abundantly above all that we ask or think
point_summary: God's ability to do "exceeding abundantly above all that we ask or think" grounds the conviction that missionary fruit belongs to God, not human effort.

context: He says very plainly in the book of Ephesians, if we're to study the entirety of the book, the first half of the book is extremely doctrinal
point_summary: Ephesians as a whole is structured so that doctrinal identity in Christ (chapters 1–3) grounds the practical obedience called for in the latter half—blessing follows faithful response, not license.

context: In Ephesians 2, 8, 9, and 10
point_summary: Faith without works is incomplete, as Paul's sequence in Ephesians 2:8–10 shows: salvation is not by works, yet believers are created unto good works as the fruit of genuine faith.

context: In Ephesians 2, 8, 9, and 10
point_summary: Salvation is by faith alone (Eph. 2:8–9), but Paul immediately follows with good works (v. 10), showing that faith and works are complementary—root and fruit—not contradictory, as James also affirms.

context: In Ephesians 2, 8, 9, and 10
point_summary: Good works are the necessary fruit of saving faith, not its root—Paul's point in Ephesians 2:10 that believers are created for good works complements rather than contradicts James's demand that faith be evidenced by works.

context: turn over to the book of Ephesians in chapter number five
point_summary: Ephesians 5:18's command "be filled with the Spirit" is presented as God's universal plan for every New Testament believer, not an optional or Pentecostal-exclusive experience.

context: in Ephesians chapter number five in verse number 18, Paul is providing instruction to these believers at Ephesus. And notice he says in verse 18, under the inspiration of the Holy Spirit of God, and be not drunk with wine, wear it as excess, but be filled with the Spirit
point_summary: Being filled with the Spirit is God's plan for every New Testament believer—not just apostles or pastors—as Paul's present passive imperative command in Ephesians 5:18 makes plain.

context: be not drunk with wine, wear it as excess, but be filled with the Spirit
point_summary: Paul's command in Ephesians 5:18 — a present passive imperative — establishes that being filled with the Spirit is God's plan and will for every New Testament believer, not only apostles or pastors.

context: be filled with the Spirit
point_summary: The present passive imperative construction of "be filled with the Spirit" reveals that Spirit-filling is God's continuous command for every believer, not a one-time experience they can produce themselves.

context: We still have the old man. That's why we read in the book of Ephesians, again, in the book of Colossians, the principle of put off and put on.
point_summary: Ephesians is cited alongside Colossians as scriptural warrant for the "put off and put on" principle, illustrating that the old nature remains after salvation and must be actively mortified.

context: in Ephesians 5, we are commanded to be filled with the spirit
point_summary: Being filled with the Spirit (Ephesians 5:18) is a divine command fulfilled when believers do their part—crucifying the flesh and yielding—while God does the filling, which pleases Him.

context: Speak the truth in love
point_summary: Speaking truth in love, not ugliness, is the Spirit-empowered boldness the sermon calls for, distinguishing right disposition from mere confrontation.

context: And be not drunk with wine when it is excess, but be filled with the Spirit
point_summary: Being filled with the Spirit, per Ephesians 5:18–20, produces praise, worship, and thankfulness to God rather than the self-centered complaining that marks an unfilled life.

context: speaking to yourselves in psalms and hymns and spiritual songs, singing and making melody in your heart to who? To the Lord
point_summary: Being filled with the Spirit produces praise directed to the Lord—psalms, hymns, and spiritual songs sung not for human applause but as evidence of Spirit-filling, in contrast to self-centered complaining.

context: singing and making melody in your heart to who? To the Lord
point_summary: Being filled with the Holy Spirit produces continual, thankful praise directed to God rather than the complaining that characterizes a self-centered life.

context: giving thanks always for all things unto God and the Father in the name of our Lord Jesus Christ
point_summary: Giving thanks "always" for "all things" (Eph. 5:20) is presented as a direct fruit of Spirit-filling, contrasted with the complaining and murmuring that characterize walking in the flesh.

context: filled with the Holy Ghost
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Love your wives, even as Christ loved the church and gave himself for it
point_summary: Ephesians 5:25's command that husbands love their wives as Christ loved the church is cited to establish that the church is the bride of Christ, identifying the Lamb's wife in Revelation 19.

context: Verse 19, Now therefore you are no more strangers and foreigners, but fellow citizens with the saints and of the household of God, and are built upon the foundation of the apostles and prophets, Jesus Christ himself being the chief cornerstone
point_summary: Ephesians 2:19–20 establishes that the apostles served as the unrepeatable foundation of the church, explaining why the twelve thrones promised in Matthew 19 belong uniquely to them.

context: Ephesians chapter number 5, a verse of Scripture

context: and be not drunk with wine wherein is excess, but be filled with the Spirit, speaking to yourselves in psalms and hymns and spiritual songs, singing and making melody in your heart to the Lord, giving thanks always for all things unto God and the Father, in the name of our Lord Jesus Christ, submitting yourselves one to another in the fear of God
point_summary: Paul's command against drunkenness in Ephesians 5:18 is grounded in the observation that "wine" is a generic biblical term whose meaning—alcoholic beverage or grape juice—must be determined by its surrounding context.

context: speak the truth in love, according to the book of Ephesians
point_summary: Speaking truth that is difficult to deliver requires doing so in love, as Paul's internal conflict over his hard letter to Corinth illustrates the tension of truth-telling described in Ephesians 4:15.

context: we're His workmanship created in Christ Jesus unto good works
point_summary: Good works in Christ are affirmed as the believer's calling, but here they are subordinated to relational reconciliation—serving God means nothing if unresolved conflict with a brother remains.

context: Unto him that is able to do exceeding abundantly above all that we ask or think according to the power that worketh in us

context: Unto him be glory where? In the church

context: we serve a God who is able to do exceeding abundantly above all that we ask or think

context: Redeem the time. Redeem the time. That we understand time is short, life is short.
point_summary: Belief in the imminent Rapture should motivate urgent, purposeful action—redeeming the time by doing the work Christ left His people here to do.

context: he might sanctify and cleanse it, speaking of the church, the bride of Christ, with the washing of water by the word, that he might present it to himself a glorious church, not having spot or wrinkle or any such thing, but that it should be holy and without blemish

context: Children, obey your parents and the Lord for this is right
point_summary: Children's obedience to parents is introduced as foundational, but the sermon's weight falls on the parental responsibility in verse 4 to raise children in nurture and admonition rather than on the children's command itself.

context: and ye fathers, provoke not your children to wrath, but bring them up in the nurture and admonition of the Lord

context: them up in the nurture and admonition
point_summary: Fathers bear a weighty responsibility to raise children through nurture—instruction aimed at increasing virtue—and admonition—gentle reproof and counseling against faults—rather than prioritizing friendship over parental correction.
